desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
In front of you stands an impressive cliff. It seems impossible to climb on it.
You search for an exit but you must give up and return by the same way
name: In Front of a Big Cliff

desc:
You can know walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You can know walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You can now walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You can know walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You can know walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You can know walk with a lot of facility on this nice path. At last, you feel a
nice wind blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering
bushes growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful ?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You are on a very tight path which goes round the cliff; you are trying to keep
your balance; the slightest false movement and you would be dead !
name: A Path around the Cliff

desc:
You are on a very steep path, obviously leading to the top of the cliff. You
feel drips of sweat in your back. You would appreciate some nice fresh air, but
nothing seems to move; you pray for some fresh wind but your requests seem to be
useless : Gods have too much to do !
name: The Steep Path

desc:
You are on a very tight path which goes round the cliff; you are trying to keep
your balance; the slightest false movement and you would be dead !
name: A Path around the Cliff

desc:
You are on a very tight path which goes round the cliff; you are trying to keep
your balance; the slightest false movement and you would be dead !
name: A Path around the Cliff

desc:
You are on a very steep path, obviously leading to the top of the cliff. You
feel drips of sweat in your back. You would appreciate some nice fresh air, but
nothing seems to move; you pray for some fresh wind but your requests seem to be
useless : Gods have too much to do !
name: The Steep Path

desc:
You are standing on a big cliff. As you look over, you notice a path going round
it. Maybe you should follow the path, rather than trying to climb over this
cliff; you surely are a better walker than alpinist !
name: A Cliff

desc:
You are standing on an earth ground. You can walk with quite a lot of facility
on this soil. But, you notice some small footprints and some fresh tracks. Maybe
you aren't alone here ? You hear some strange noises in the bushes right next to
you; what was that animal which just passed between your legs ! Too late, he was
just too fast for you.
name: Path among the Bushes

desc:
You notice the bushes define a quite perfect circle around you. At your right,
you notice a small path. The bushes are so high that you can't see what is
behind them. You hear some voices; you try to shout, but your voice is shallowed
by the noise of the wind !
name: A Small Glade

desc:
You are standing on an earth ground. You can walk with quite a lot of facility
on this soil. But, you notice some small footprints and some fresh tracks. Maybe
you aren't alone here ? You hear some strange noises in the bushes right next to
you; what was that animal which just passed between your legs ! Too late, he was
just too fast for you.
name: Path among the Bushes

desc:
You are standing on an earth ground. You can walk with quite a lot of facility
on this soil. But, you notice some small footprints and some fresh tracks. Maybe
you aren't alone here ? You hear some strange noises in the bushes right next to
you; what was that animal which just passed between your legs ! Too late, he was
just too fast for you.
name: Path among the Bushes

desc:
You are standing on a small plateau. You take a look around you, but notice
nothing special except the small wood far west. The wind blows quite strongly
here and you feel exposed to the wild nature elements ! You should find a better
place to rest.
name: On a Plateau

desc:
You are on a small earth path leading down. You notice that this path must be
used quite often because you see no grass on the middle of it. You can smell the
fresh air coming up to you.
name: A Path Going Down

desc:
You are at a sharp bend in the path. To the west you can see an extremely open
area among the bushes, where no trees seem to grow. To the north the trail
descends sharply, making its way between the dense green shrubs which cling
tenaciously to life even on such a slope.
name: A Bend in the Path

desc:
You are standing on the top of the pile. You try to keep your balance. Maybe you
should get down as fast as possible. The trunks may roll down...
name: On the Trunks

desc:
You are on a very tight path which goes round the cliff; you are trying to keep
your balance; the slightest false movement and you would be dead !
name: A Path around the Cliff

desc:
You are standing on a small plateau. You take a look around you, but notice
nothing special except the small wood far west. The wind blows quite strongly
here and you feel exposed to the wild nature elements ! You should find a better
place to rest.
name: On a Plateau

desc:
You are on a small earth path leading down. You notice that this path must be
used quite often because you see no grass on the middle of it. You can smell the
fresh air coming up to you.
name: A Path Going Down

desc:
Amongst the low, undulating hills of the downs, the great hedge stretches into
the west and the east, hugging the contours of the land. On its southern side,
this ragged trail winds, keeping with a few score yards of the bushes. The grass
has in many places overgrown the track but the trace of many former travellers
has not been erased completely.
name: The Hedge-trail

desc:
This is a hilly region lying below the impressive face of the Milkfall Edge.
This side of the down, the broken face of limestone is exposed, its pale colour
giving the escarpment its name. Directly below the face, the terrain is littered
with irregular blocks of rock which have fallen from the eroded cliff. These
range from pebble size to the size of a house, making travel both treacherous
and unpredictable. However, away from the Edge, most of the region is covered in
grasses which thrive on the downs.
name: Below the Milkfall Edge

desc:
Within a mile of the escarpment, the Milkfall Edge dominates the view east. Its
pale stone creates a long chain of rock stretching south, but broken here by a
deep cutting right through to the backslope.
name: Below the Milkfall Edge

desc:
You are standing in a dense forest, just along the road. You see a small path to
the north, but the forest seems to continue even further. The air is fresh and
an agreeable scent of pine floats around you. You feel relaxed and walk joyfully
in the forest singing some old songs.
name: The Lower Forest

desc:
You are standing in a dense forest, just along the road. You see a small path to
the north, but the forest seems to continue even further. The air is fresh. An
agreeable scent of pine floats around you. You feel relaxed as you talk joyfully
in the forest.
name: The Lower Forest

desc:
You are standing in a dense forest, just along the road. You see a small path to
the north, but the forest seems to continue even further. The air is fresh and
an agreeable scent of pine floats around you. You feel relaxed and walk joyfully
in the forest singing some old songs.
name: The Lower Forest

desc:
Compared to the green carpeting of the downs to the west, the thin, rocky soil
here is a dramatic change in environment. It is the hardiest of grasses which
grow in patches, while short bushes dot the landscape, most notably some
spindle-trees. Exposed rocks coated in grey lichen are common, while slimy
trails show that slugs and snails must roam at night. Occasional droppings show
that large animals frequent the plains, while field pipers occasionally swoop
low overhead.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
The trail obviously still has use, as the sparse grasses have been kept from
overgrowing the meandering path by the scouring of hoof and foot. Due to this
wear, the trail is some one foot lower than the surrounding plains. Stones,
cleared from the trail long ago, still mark the width of the trail. However,
much of the line of stones has been disturbed over the years.
name: Trail to Central Cardolan

desc:
The trail heads southwest from the northeastern border of the South Downs, into
the heart of the former Dunedain realm which these days is mainly deserted.
Though commerce along this track is probably scarce, its route into the southern
lands still makes it a minor artery in the network of roads and trails which
were established many thousands of years ago.
name: Trail to Central Cardolan

desc:
North-east of the Milkfall Edge, the great hedge of the former Dunedain realm
bends here from its normal east-west course. The trail also follows the hedge's
course, though the north-eastern leg has all but vanished due to lack of use.
name: Bend in the Hedge-trail

desc:
The wind murmurs as one crosses the endless miles of grass-covered prairie. The
soil here is capable of sustaining quite a variety of docks and grasses, quite
unlike the majority of the Lone-lands. The ground slopes upward to the west
where the last of the escarpments of the South Downs breaks.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
East of the last escarpment of the Tyrn Hyarmen, the soil here is healthy enough
to support lush grasses and flowering plants. Further east, this is no longer
true: the plains of the Lone-lands start and with them a harsher environment.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The trail and the hedge here pass a few miles to the north of the termination of
the Milkfall edge, the last of the South Downs. The terrain at this point is
elevated due to its proximity to the escarpment, providing a sloping gradient
down to the north where the East Road crosses the land.
name: The Hedge-trail

desc:
This is the most easterly of the South Downs: the Milkfall Edge. West is the
edge, the limestone exposed on the escarpment itself. Some three score yards
below the edge lie many blocks which have broken away from the face. The slope
is covered in grass, providing good grazing for sheep owned by any shepherd in
these downs.
name: The Milkfall Edge

desc:
Cutting through the down, the valley is rugged and strewn with blocks of
limestone that have tumbled from the steep sides. As to why the valley developed
through the scarp, perhaps the reason is it originally was the site of a vein of
rock less resistant to erosion. Whatever the reason though, this is a haven for
plants and animals in the South Downs. The uneven floor of the valley is covered
in bushes with broad leaves to make the most of the poor light levels the valley
provides. These leaves seem to appeal to small deer which shift uneasily at ones
presence. Droppings also suggest others mammals.
name: Narrow Valley

desc:
Here you find yourself in a large room built at the base of the tower. Great
beams of sturdy oak arch upwards from the stone walls to form a conical ceiling.
Comfortable chairs lie around a carved table allowing guests to rest. An
impressive door made of silvery metal stands facing north and is the only
visible entrance to the tower. Built into the west wall are stairs which rise up
to a door marked with runes. The stairs also spiral down into the floor from
which a soft glow emerges. A large sign stands above a warm fireplace set into
the north wall.
name: The Entrance Hall

desc:
This is little more than a low ridge facing north-south in the plains. Rising
only a dozen yards in height, it is thrice this in width and thrice as long as
it is wide. Small bushes grow on both sides of the ridge, but none on top.
Centred on the flattened top, towards the southern end, stands the shell of a
tower, made of a white stone.
name: The Windswept Ridge

desc:
Within the monotony of the windswept plains, three trails come together at the
site of a flat rock. There is no odour on the air carried by the wind, not even
the rural smells of ordure. The centre of Eriador is a pretty bleak place.
name: Meeting of the Trails

desc:
This is the Oiolad, the endless plain which forms the flat basin at the centre
of Eriador. Circulating it are evidence of upheaval and subsequent weathering.
For instance the South Downs to the west and the Weather Hills north-west show
that great forces have been at work in Eriador in the past. North there are
open, flat lands as far as the North Downs, while northeast is the shelf which
cradled the old kingdom of Angmar. East, there are no such major summits until
the Ettendales. Many miles of travel lay ahead for any traveller to those sites
from this spot.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
The soil is thin, and what there is is poor. The underlying limestone has meant
that most of the water disappears straight underground through numerous cracks.
Patches of grass try and bind the soil as best they can in this alkaline
environment. Elsewhere, the ground is blown away by winds which have little to
impede their progress. This situation seems similar in all directions, for at
least several miles.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Here amongst the empty lands, the scattered remains of a farm lie. Some measure
of its boundaries can be estimated from the still orderly border of rock piles:
the age-old remains of low stone walls. Very little evidence of a farm building
remains, perhaps it was made of wood? However, though this is the Lone-lands,
some visitors have stayed at the farm recently. Boot-prints mark the ground, but
no more can be determined without closer examination. Further afield, beyond the
farm, the rugged land of the En Eredoriath stretches for miles in any direction.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Though the land is quite flat, on short scales it is broken and rough to travel
upon. This trail, though no more than a cleared, well-trodden route, provides a
simpler passage for those coming from the lands north or south. Some sunken
depressions of land away from the trail hold the promise of water. However, all
they contain is algae-ridden, stagnant pools, their edges host to sedge and
nettle. The Lone-lands are not noted for their hospitality.
name: Trail through the Plains

desc:
The infertile land holds few edible treasures, so the traveller needs plentiful
supplies to cross the deserted plains. This land was not always so empty, and
the occasional outline of the foundations of a building show that once it must
have been possible to survive here, even if a hardship. Even the mighty fortress
of Arnor no longer stands upon Weathertop.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Amongst the windswept flatlands of the Oiolad, this track passes. Birds live in
the wastes, a few sizeable bushes housing a nest or the remains of one. The
shorter plants that manage to grow have covered the ground, but the old ruts of
the track can still guide the eye.
name: Bend in an Overgrown Track

desc:
In the open and eroded lands of the En Eredoriath, a ravine crosses. From its
steep bluffs, a winding track is cut down to the river below. To the west, it
heads towards the South Downs, where the grass is greener. A rusted hoop of iron
and a tumbled pile of limestone blocks at the top of the cutting suggest some
previous industry from long ago.
name: Track above the Ravine

desc:
In the flat lands the eroded soil reveals the underlying limestone landscape.
Crumbs of soil and organic matter are bound together by clumps of grass which
barely flourish. Lichens dot the surface of the bare rock, champions in the
alkaline environment. Animals are obviously rare.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
The river flows on to the south, carrying its waters collected from the
Lone-lands. Hemmed in by the steep bluffs of flinty limestone, the river is
channelled into a narrow and deep course. There are no banks to speak of so in
the splashed band only mosses and small plants grow. Under the water, long
fronds of green waterweed hang like hair in the breeze of the current. Within
its clear depths, small black fish dart, while the occasional flash of silver
suggests larger life.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
Hemmed in by the ravine in which the river flows, one has little option but to
let the waters carry oneself downstream or fight the current and make upstream.
If it were not for this flow, one might be able to climb the steep sides. This
is impossible however, as the walls of the ravine are mossy and slippery. Fish
swim close to the surface, sometimes tantalisingly close. If one had a rod and
line, this would make an excellent days fishing, but maybe this is not the day.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The Nen-i-Sul, or in the tongue of men, the Weatherwater bends. The ruins of a
small port remain at this turning. On the outer bank of the bend, piles of
ancient stones denote the stilts upon which a dock once stood. A poor choice
perhaps, to build the berthing place on the point of the river where the current
runs fastest. However, it was not built without thought as one can scramble
ashore and follow a winding trail which climbs out of the ravine.
name: Ancient Berth on the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
This great fissure is long. It is very long. Stretching for miles in both
directions, one has little choice but to carry on the quest to reach the end.
The uneven nature of the tunnel makes the journey even more tiresome. The ground
is slippery from the water that collects in small pools making an unusual source
of drinking water. Beside the path that is cut into the fissure, mining
equipment has been discarded. It must have been left here years ago, as the
mineral-laden waters have coated them in a deposit of stone, making them strange
additions to the underworld.
name: Orchabal's Fissure

desc:
This great scar definitely has an end, but it cannot be seen. The fissure is not
without its features either. At one point the fissure might twist slightly, at
others the roof comes perilously close before climbing again into the dark. Who
would have thought that such a marvel existed below the wastes of the
Lone-lands? Though the fissure seems for the main devoid of any side-exits
beyond small hollows, here there is a sizeable opening in the southern wall.
name: Orchabal's Fissure

desc:
The cavern formed in the rock, seems to be the result of the collapse of some
kind. From the subsequent deposits of minerals on the surfaces it must have been
some time ago, but there is a slab of rock as tall as a tower which has broken
away from one wall, only to rest its upper end on another.
name: Limestone Cave

desc:
Mile upon mile of the fissure stretch away beyond your vision. Within this long
flaw in the limestone shelf, the tunnel weaves its way. The miners have felt it
necessary to support the ceiling in several places with stout wooden props.
However, when you touch them, you notice that the wood has been calcified by the
supply of mineral-laden water. Now the props are as much a part of the rocky
interior as any other stalagmite or limestone drapery.
name: Orchabal's Fissure

desc:
This is a great fissure, yet it narrows to a crack just west of here. The
limestone in which the crack appeared long ago shows all the signs of gradual
mineral deposition due to the laden waters which have percolated from above:
stalactites, stalagmites, flowstone etc. One important feature that has survived
unscathed is a brass plaque attached to the northern wall next to a passage that
is hewn into the rock.
name: End of Orchabal's Fissure

desc:
Roughly hewn is this passage, stretching many hundreds of yards from north to
south. The majority of the rock through which the tunnel passes is limestone, a
major component of both Weathertop and the central plains of Eriador. Water has
over the centuries dripped into the passage, creating stubby stalagmites.
name: The Long Passageway

desc:
An entrance to a passage is on the stair, the stairway briefly widening to
provide a stopping-place where climbers can catch their breath. The stairs
winding down are coated in dust and powered rock. However, the stairs leading up
are in a far worse state, great chunks of flinty rock choking the route.
name: Landing on the Long Stair

desc:
The sounds of ones footsteps ring off the walls and cast echoes from the
surrounding hallways. There is little dust on the ground to muffle this sound,
unlike the dusty stairwell to the south. The passages north and east are cut
from the rock of the hill itself: limestone, though it has been dutifully
smoothed. The floor, although not level, is even enough for a conduit which
these days sees virtually no traffic.
name: A Junction

desc:
Walking through this smoothly hewn tunnel is akin to stepping back in time to
see the progress of the race of man. There are carvings along the wall and
scenes of early man hunting and making camp, fire-making and celebrating. The
frieze must surely predate the Dunedain and possibly even the time of the
Numenoreans. Many hands must have once passed along the walls, feeling the
works, their torches scorching and blackening the upper walls.
name: An Ancient Tunnel

desc:
The dark tunnel from the west comes to an abrupt halt beside a wooden door,
which is built into the north wall. The door has not coped well with the passing
years, appearing old and mostly rotten. Markings and carvings once covered the
door, but they have been ravaged just like the portal.
name: End of the Ancient Tunnel

desc:
Once perhaps this was some antechamber to a tomb, but vandals have sacked what
goods were placed here. However, the paintings on the wall seem unmarked. A lord
is shown in the various stages of entombment and his spirit's subsequent leaving
of the body and taking gifts towards the sun in the sky. The floor is of hard
rock, in the most part roughly hewn. The exception is the polished path of
inlaid stone that curves from the doorway to the narrow chamber west.
name: The Sacked Chamber

desc:
A rectangular plinth stands at the centre of this small room, creating a narrow
space between the coffin on its top and the dark walls. The top of the coffin is
broken in two, only the lower half remaining. The upper half lies on the floor
disregarded. An engraving on the top has writing in a strange alphabet. The
language seems akin to a distant form of Westron. All one can make out is the
largest word, best translated to mean Ralgund. The coffin proper is empty. If
this was where the remains of some lord or king were inured, they have since
decayed or been looted.
name: The Tomb of Ralgund

desc:
The passage has been cut through the rock of the hill in a roughly circular
cross-section, no extra effort being undertaken to dress the stone. A few
shallow depressions hold some milky subterranean water.
name: Hewn Passageway

desc:
The subterranean passage bends abruptly at this place. Diagonally across the
passage from the inner corner to the outer, an archway has been chiselled. There
is water on the floor, collected in shallow pools, but it is murky in
appearance, and not fit for drinking.
name: The Hewn Passageway Bends

desc:
The roughly hewn path curves around the walls of the Deep. Though not as muddy
as the ledge to the north, water creeps down the walls, across the ledge and
trickles down into the depths.
name: The Ledge of Theddin's Deep

desc:
The architectural theme of the passage changes along its length. To the east,
the passage is hewn from the rock and nothing more. In contrast, at this western
end the stone has been meticulously worked. Limestone blocks dress the floor,
walls and ceiling. While those of the floor are plain, the blocks of the walls
depict an axe head motif: each block is the size and shape of a twin- bladed
axe, set diagonally, details residing on each stone. The wall is broken to the
north as great inscribed archway leads to a great chamber.
name: Before Athoin's Vault

desc:
This is a marvellous cavern of great height, higher indeed than it is wide. The
rock seems to be limestone, but the walls are imbedded with mica or quartz. A
dim glow seems to light the interior of the crystals, casting the large cave in
an eerie light. This light is refracted in the rain of drops that falls from
many places in the vaulting of the cavern. The walls are slick with this water
as it runs down to the muddy floor. The mud of this floor is a mix of fine
grains of rock, suspended in a watery solution. This seems a marvellous place
and it is no wonder that Athoin sought to protect it. However awe-inspiring this
place might be, one gets the subtle feeling that one shouldn't tarry as the
direction of north and south become blurred. Perhaps in some ways, the spirit of
the dwarf lives on, or maybe there is some enchantment in the walls.
name: The Vault of Athoin

desc:
The constant drip-drip of water reverberates within the great chamber. Diffuse
light illuminates the far walls as well as the great heights, encrusted with
pale crystal. This wonder is in contrast to the mineral-laden mud around ones'
legs that flows mysteriously, and a strange feeling of disorientation.
name: The Vault of Athoin

desc:
Between Athoin's Vault and the deep cave of his father Theddin, stands this
ledge. The mud from the Vault covers the rock, making the area treacherous.
Directly east is the centre of the cavern: the Deep. The bottom is some way
below, while its ceiling is lost in the darkness above. Since the cave is only
illuminated by the radiance from the vault, its size can not be determined,
though it is certainly large. Grey-green lichens grow on the limestone where the
light falls.
name: The Ledge of Theddin's Deep

desc:
Lying below the hill, remote from the outside world, lies this room dedicated to
arcane research. The plaster walls are lined with shelves holding ageing
scrolls, books, and manuscripts. In the centre of the room is a grand pine
table, with numerous pieces of documentation resting on it. Dark stains mark the
lightly coloured wood while next to some beakers stand irregular fragments of
black crystal. Around the table, several strong wooden chairs provide a modicum
of comfort, even though they are rather worn and threadbare.
name: Research Chamber

desc:
The ledge around the great cave begins and ends here in the northeast corner of
the Deep. A thick column of limestone is fused with the cavern wall to the south
and the ledge does not continue around the obstruction. Rather, set into the
column is a heavy door made from ancient oak, bound in iron and set with a lock.
From beneath the door, a yellow light leaks out, illuminating the ledge.
name: The Ledge before the Door

desc:
Maintaining a constant elevation, the ledge continues along the edge of the
Deep. The sounds of drips roundabout ring in the confines of the chamber, while
down below water gurgles. A shape occasionally moves out in the wide space of
the cavern, but never close enough to be identified.
name: The Ledge of Theddin's Deep

desc:
The limestone shelf curves around the girth of the Deep, providing safe passage.
Though slick with rivulets of water from above, the ledge is artificially
roughened by notches, providing the much-needed traction. Low overhangs have
been also pruned, but only to man height, so those taller must stoop as they
edge their way along.
name: The Ledge of Theddin's Deep

desc:
This track in the wilderness is barely visible through lack of use. If it was
not for the inhospitable soil, plants would have totally obliterated its path.
As it is, one can walk on the track east or west amongst the weeds and grasses.
name: Overgrown Track

desc:
The route of this track bends though one sees this only through keen
observation. Stumbling through these lands, a traveller would be forgiven for
missing this nuance in a long-dead trail.
name: Bend in an Overgrown Track

desc:
You descend the spiral stairs from the hall above into a long hallway whose name
is all too appropriate. The length of this arrased corridor is lit by a variety
of lamps fixed to the north wall which illuminate a dozen large woven pictures.
The tapestries depict the history of the grandson of Radorac. The first hanging
in the eastern hall shows the isle of Numenor in its last days, Armenelos
wreathed in black smoke whilst a great fleet of ships are armed in the havens of
Romenna. Further tapestries show the sailing of the great Armada towards
Valinor, Radorac II standing on the flagship Alcarondas. Towards the east end of
the hall is shown the landings of Ar-Pharazon and his small party on Tuna, and
their entombment in the Caves of the Forgotten at the changing of the world. In
the final weaves in the western hall, by Manwe's decree the tomb is opened and
Pharazon, Radorac II and Septumar are forced to sail to Endor to atone for their
crimes. The eastern hall ends at an arched portal above which a plaque stands. A
similar plaque marks the western door of iron. In front of this door a valet
stands guard awaiting his master.
name: The Great Weavehall

desc:
This room is a truly fitting resting-place for a great treasure. Basalt walls
curve up to a high ceiling. At the apex of the ceiling, a star of lapis lazuli
sends its fronds out to touch all the corners of the chamber. The porphyry floor
is surmounted by a glass dais at its centre, whilst pulsing crystals set around
the walls illuminate the vault. An ivory plaque is fixed to the dais.
name: The Resting place of the Palantir

desc:
Greys and blacks dominate this rectangular room. The floor is of slate, as are
the walls. The ceiling some dozen feet above, is flat and dressed with dark
schists. Rather than use wood, the builders have erected great timbers of slate,
the stone crossing the ceiling. Curious for this complex, none of the surfaces
are decorated. The only features of the room stand next to the exits. A large
statue in basalt depicts a lion guarding the eastern exit.
name: Before the Sentinel

desc:
Brass shelves lining the room hold a considerable store of knowledge. The books
are on a variety of subjects but all of them deemed valuable enough to preserve
underground for future generations. Desks and chairs are provided for perusal of
the tomes, each having a bright stone for reading by.
name: A Small Library

desc:
Here the large corridor ends at the bottom of a set of stairs leading up. Large
blocks of stone rest on the stairs and on the floor and round a gaping hole in
the floor. Perhaps some earthquake created the crack and blocked the stairs many
years ago. Perhaps the upheaval is the result of great and destructive magics,
who knows? At any rate, a prospect of an exit looks bleak.
name: Bottom of the Collapsed Stairs

desc:
You can now walk with ease on this nice path. At last, you feel a nice wind
blowing in your hair. You can smell the fine perfume of the flowering bushes
growing next to you; a sensation of freedom fills your body. Isn't life
beautiful?
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
South of here is the eastern end of the barren lands called the south downs, a
long stretch of sand where few living creatures and plants will be found. The
earth around here is more hard soil, brown and cracked. The hedge grows fairly
thin a few yards north of here, with large burnt areas for some reason.
name: Hedge Trail

desc:
This is where the downs seem to end. You can see the hedge far north, while the
hilly, sandy plains stretch out to the west. The snakes and worms around this
place are not exactly the sort of company most people prefer, and the area seems
desolate and uninhabited.
name: The Edge

desc:
In front of you, you notice a pile of old tree trunks. There seems to be no more
woods in this part of the region; green bushes spread overall.
name: The Old Tree Trunks

desc:
Water bubbles out from an unreachable ledge to the west. The water meanders down
a limestone wall to collect in a small pool on the far side of the cave. The
ground is covered with muddy sediment and rocks but gets progressively drier as
it leads up a small natural stair to the outside world.
name: Watery Cave

desc:
This thin scar underground has become the passage for water which flows down the
western face from above. Water also pours from a thin crack to the west where it
has found a route through the limestone. These collected waters of the hill
disappear down a narrow tunnel east. Sediment and rocks have formed at the
lowest level, and it is quite slippery, the waters threatening to pitch one down
this slide.
name: The Bottom of Theddin's Deep

desc:
The ledge ends abruptly on the southern wall of the Deep, the shelf of rock
disappearing to the east. Unlike the rest of the ledge one has encountered, the
rock here is not slick with water. Only condensation prevents the limestone from
drying out. The splash of water can be heard clearly from below.
name: End of the Ledge

desc:
You notice that the road seems to continue to the north. As you look to the
south you notice a small depression. There seems to be no trees around; only
green flourishing bushes grow peacefully there; you are enveloped by a
undefinable scent: so beautiful, but so strong that you barely can stand it.
What a shame...
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
You are standing on the most popular road of Middle-earth. There seems to be no
real difficulty to walk on this well prepared road. You feel quite safe on this
road, but you shiver as you look around you. You wish you'd never have to quit
this road...the outside world is so frightening !
name: Old East Road

desc:
You notice that the road seems to continue to the north. As you look to the
south you notice a small depression. There seems to be no trees around; only
green flourishing bushes grow peacefully there; you are enveloped by a
undefinable scent: so beautiful, but so strong that you barely can stand it.
What a shame...
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road keeps going east and west. A very sizable hill can be seen off in the
west. To the south, the trees gradually give way to fields, and to the north, a
thick hedgerow obstructs movement. The dirt on the road is smooth and firm from
constant foot, hoof, and cart travel.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road turns gradually to the east and south. An approximately four hundred
feet tall hill to the west obscures the view. A pasture with rich green grass is
located at the foot of the hill to the west, while the hedgerow to the north
blocks further movement in that direction.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Between fields to the southeast and the foot of the Bree-hill to the northwest,
the road gently swings east and south. Wooden fences divide the fields into
small lots. Each field is growing a different crop, giving the landscape an
extremely vibrant colour. Due to regular use, the road's dirt is hard and
smooth.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
The road gently curves east and south. A sizable dike and a towering hedge that
appear to comprise Bree's natural defences run side by side to the west of the
road. While a field of vivid barley is neatly delineated by a wooden fence to
the southeast, the road is inching closer to the dike as it travels south.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Green grass and a few plants dot the ground of the pasture. The hedge that
delineates Bree's eastern border begins to grow tremendously thick and tall to
the west. A road does progressively curve around the base of Bree-hill to the
east and south.
name: Foot of Bree-hill

desc:
This park provides a quiet respite from the noise of the city. The grass is
well-manicured, with some low rolling highs sprawling in every direction. A
short path connects the street to a clearing in the grass here. A multitude of
insects teem the grass, feeding on scraps left by city residents. Linked with
the wall to the east, a tower rises to the south.
name: Tranquil Grassy Park

desc:
Enclosed on the southern and eastern side, with thick logs of standing pine to
the south and thinner wood with stone behind to the east. Along the eastern
wall, six large targets made from packed straw stand out with their painted red
circlular stripes. On the ground, immersed white logs mark out three different
distances to the targets.
name: Archery Range

desc:
The base of the tower guarding the east gate of Bree is made from large stones,
stacked up high into the air and reinforced with metal plates and sacks of dirt
against the south and east walls. Along the east wall a stone staircase rises
into a level above, and west several rooms can be seen, spreading a pleasant
aroma of food cooking.
name: Base of the Guard Tower

desc:
A wide landing in the middle of the stairs leading up spreads out west, a thick
wooden floor holding the office where the Bree guards are administered. The
southern wall in small maps of Bree and the surrounding areas. Along the eastern
wall the stairs lead down towards the east gate and up to the tower arms
storage.
name: Tower Administrative Office

desc:
A solid wooden landing covered with a thick reed rug spreads out from where the
stairs from the administrative office come up. Solid metal lockers are placed in
lines throughout, holding armour and weapons for the Bree guards. Any new guard
arriving for service is equipped here before heading out for their daily
station.
name: Tower Armament

desc:
About eleven feet in diameter, a round floor with a stone railing allows for a
view of the Bree eastern wilderness. Wild animals, travellers and disturbing
elements are under constant watch by vigilant guards, allowing a rapid response
to any threat. A light wooden hatch, with a solid metal handle, leads down into
the tower. Beside the hatch, a hole allows yelling alerts into the towers main
rooms.
name: Top of a Guard Tower

desc:
This space is a comfortable tunnel-like hall. Although built into the ground,
this hobbit hole shows no signs of the dirty, oozy, muddy dwelling an
uncivilised being might abide in. This room shows that many hours of care and
work have been spent in order to make the place as comfortable as possible for
the owner and his guests. The wooden wall to the south has a small peephole
installed in it.
name: Tidy Hobbit Hole

desc:
Over twenty feet wide a wooden roof covered in stone shingles covers the entire
training yard. A wooden horse meant to teach children riding, large plaques of
animals tracks and a wooden wall with attached metal spikes for climbing are
spread out, all made to teach wilderness skills. Some solid tents for living are
standing along the eastern wall.
name: Woodsman's Roofed Yard

desc:
Over forty yards long, the roof over the saw is supported by logs every two
yards, each wide and solid and over four yards tall. Built as a larger copy of
the Old Mill in Hobbiton, yet here the water is used to power a saw. Studs and
planks for the Bree builders are being produced by sawyers daily.
name: Long Dusty Sawmill

desc:
The trail is even more worn out here than to the west. Ruts from carts and
carriages are several inches deep. No longer well maintained, this section of
trail is completely saturated with footprints, human and animal. Several fresh
and rotting crops are strewn along the ground here and trail off south towards
the crop storage building.
name: Worn Logging Trail

desc:
A massive iron gate to the north guards the entrance to the log preparation
site. A large storage building to the north stands a short distance behind the
gate. The ruts are quite deep on the trail from the frequent and continuous
travel by huge carts that can hold a considerable amount of heavy logs.
name: Deeply-worn Logging Trail

desc:
The path bends around the logging trail past the log preparation site. Farm
equipment lays about the ground. A cart and plough are here waiting to be used
in the ploughing of the fields. Several pigs play in their pen and are wrought
with mud. There is a trough filled with water for the animals to drink from.
Standing beside the trough is an iron water pump that can be used by weary
travellers and workers.
name: Bend in the Path

desc:
The worn path creates a cross shape as it is regularly used to move farming and
logging equipment to their respective points. The smells are a mishmash of dust,
dirt, and mud, along with various types of wood and bark. The wash of previous
rains has created some crevices in the earth. Small pieces of tree and log
litter are scattered about the ground.
name: Worn Crossroad

desc:
Countless woven sacks are stacked along the walls up to twelve feet high,
including in the room's centre. The sacks are stuffed to the brim with crops
that were harvested from the farmer's fields. Several small tools are hung on
the southern wall. Wheelbarrows are laid about strategically for moving the
sacks around the storage area.
name: Crop Storage

desc:
Despite looking dilapidated on the outside, the shed is in surprisingly good
shape on the inside. Some stairs leading to the second level look fairly worn.
There are a few wooden log stores here, packed with cut firewood and stumps. A
huge logging axe is stuck in the centre of a massive stump in the middle of the
shed. The windows are cracked and broken, with dust thick enough that one may
never see through them again. An old plough is rusting away in the corner.
name: Logging Shed

desc:
The flooring of the attic is almost completely covered with dried-up, caked-on
mud. There are soggy wooden planks all about the floor and several small window
frames are left open to allow for a slight draft. The roof rises nearly five
feet above the planking. Near the attic's west end, the ceiling lifts back
upward, angled into the next room. Spider webs line the area in nearly every
crevice, and many rat droppings are spread all over the area.
name: Musty Old Attic

desc:
The room looks well lived in. Decomposed leaf litter and small twigs are piled
up towards the window, which looks to have been broken long ago and has a sharp
shard of glass jutting out from the side. There are three bedframes which are
rusted, and all of the mattresses look stained and dirty. Red paint has long
since chipped and fallen from the walls. Standing in the centre of the room is a
single chair, without a seat, and a trash can.
name: Farmhands' Quarters

desc:
Several huge carts, loaded with considerably large, heavy hardwood logs arrive
and depart from this location daily. Logs that arrive here are completely clean
and free from branches and debris. The sliding wall of this building is usually
closed, protecting the wood and workers from the possibility of harsh weather.
The smell of freshly cut wood permeates the air.
name: Log Preparation Site

desc:
Within this large open-air building, several weighty hardwood logs have been
placed here, roughly, in piles. Other stacks of smaller logs are scattered
throughout the area. There is tree bark littered all over the ground, creating a
soft yet splintery floor to walk on. The logs have had their branches removed
and are placed in smaller, separate piles. The greens of the forest create a
harsh contrast with the various browns of logs in the area.
name: Log Collection Area

desc:
A large path through the forest has been created to allow workers to transport
logs to the gathering site, which is housed in a large open-air building to the
south. This is the most intensively used area of the region's woodlands, and the
surrounding wood is littered with stumps and sapling trees.
name: Combe Forest

desc:
The path forks north and east, and beyond its edges, a large patch of forest is
cut down to stumps. There are many fallen trees on the ground, some of which
have had their branches removed and have been prepared for transport toward the
log collection site.
name: Path through a Clear-cut Forest

desc:
The southern forest path ends at the door of the famous Blue Trout Inn. To the
east, trees have been planted in equally spaced rows to make harvesting easier.
The faint creaking of branches can be heard all around the area, and the
rustling of leaves adds a hush to the air. There is a sign here, marking the
entryway into the Blue Trout Inn.
name: Inn in the Combe Forest

desc:
On the path to the Combe Farmhouse, the trail is well-worn but has never been
made permanent. The grass folds over itself on the path's sides as it has long
since worn out in the centre. Pebbles and stones line the way towards the house,
and when walked upon, the sound of crunching can be heard underfoot.
name: Farmhouse Path

desc:
Candlelight from above fills the room with bright golden light. The hallways are
immaculately clean despite the dirty nature of the work being performed outside.
A painting of cattle is hung on the wall above the fireplace which is filling
the home with warmth. The smell of burning lumber and smoke saturates the
residence. Wooden pillars, which appear to be load-bearing in nature, look
gritty and aged. The wood floor seems to creak and crack under the pressure of
those who would walk by.
name: Farmer's House

desc:
The Blue Trout Inn is a trendy meeting and resting place for travellers who come
to the village of Combe. The building is made entirely out of wood and logs
sourced from the local forest. There is a strong smell of sap inside, and the
windows appear to be a little more than aged. The inn consists of seven sparsely
furnished guest rooms with beds of sufficient quality, and linens that look to
be clean and well taken care of. A wooden sign is hung behind the bar.
name: Blue Trout Inn

desc:
The hardwood trees in this part of the forest have been planted much closer
together than the others, resulting in a thicker canopy above the forest floor.
The area has yet to be touched by the loggers as they rotate through the forest
sections each year, allowing the others to be regrown. Several animals and birds
have taken advantage of this and have found refuge and solace here, in the
meantime.
name: Combe Forest

desc:
Entering through the small door, you come to this impressive oval chamber,
panelled with stout woods. A large circular table made of elm dominates the
room. Five high-backed chairs face the table and at each, the wood of the table
is marked with a rune. Directly opposite the only doors a large hearth burns.
Windows in the chamber face to all the points of the compass and provide the
only visible means of lighting during the day. A soft breeze blows through the
room making the atmosphere more bearable. This wind catches the rising smoke
from the fire, curling it around as it makes its way up into the centre oculus
of the conical roof.
name: The Council of the Dunedain

desc:
A large open room with smaller compartments along the southern and northern
walls spreads out, with tools for the fur handling trade on benches and tables.
The compartments along the northern wall seem to be used for tanning and shaping
furs, while the southern ones are for coarser work like preservation, salting,
and unhairing. Suspended from the ceiling hangs a large skin, with red letters
on it like a sign.
name: Tanner's Workshop
